title: Umbra Loft noir concert-series poster art: three archival live shots driven to a matched two-tone amber/ink duotone + halftone treatment for print and social
grounding_note: VERBATIM from the Freelancer.com "MidSight Noir" brief: a "luxury-inspired creative studio specializing in high-impact digital design, branding, and visual storytelling" that is "Built on a dark aesthetic philosophy" and turns "ideas into bold, minimal, and visually powerful identities"; signature services include "Premium Graphic Designing (posters, banners, social media creatives)" and "Professional Photo & Video Editing (cinematic, color-graded visuals)." REASONABLY ENRICHED into a runnable Adobe-connector-only benchmark matching the slot (duotone_poster_fx / edit / arts_culture_music): the studio's own dark-aesthetic "posters" + "cinematic, color-graded" photo-editing service is re-cast as a concrete client job for a fictional live-jazz venue, "Umbra Loft," which hands off three flat, dull archival performance photos and asks for a cohesive noir two-tone (duotone) poster treatment so the three read as one concert-series set. Pure EDIT: supplied photos are transformed (prep -> desaturate -> two-ink duotone -> halftone -> grain -> vignette-style edge darkening via whole-image tools -> crop) into flat print-ready and social-ready image deliverables. No layout composed, no InDesign/Illustrator template bound, and no new content generated: NO generative fill, NO generative-expand/outpaint, NO text-to-image / from-scratch art or logo, NO upscaling, NO object-removal-with-reconstruction. Numeric values (amber/ink duotone RGB pair, halftone radius, crop ratios, poster dimensions) are enrichment chosen to be concrete and gradeable.
one_line_ask: Take three flat archival live-performance photos and drive all three to a matched noir two-tone (amber-highlight / deep-ink-shadow) duotone treatment with halftone texture and film grain, then deliver each as a print-ready poster-ratio image and a square social crop.
full_brief: Umbra Loft, a fictional basement live-jazz and spoken-word venue, is announcing its autumn "Midnight Sessions" concert series and wants a cohesive poster look built entirely from its own archive. They are handing off three raw archival performance photos pulled from past nights: a stand-up double-bass player under a single stage spot, a vocalist leaning into a vintage ribbon mic, and a wide shot of the trio mid-set on the low stage. As-shot the photos are unusable for a series: each is flat and dull, shot on a phone in near-dark with mixed warm house light and a cool spill from an EXIT sign, slightly tilted, with a cluttered background of cabling, monitors and audience heads. The venue's whole identity is dark, moody and cinematic, and they want the three photos driven to one signature NOIR TWO-TONE (duotone) treatment so they read instantly as a matched set at poster size on the venue wall and at Instagram-thumbnail size in the feed: deep ink-blue shadows lifting to a warm amber highlight, a subtle print-screen halftone texture so it feels like a screened gig poster, fine film grain so the flat inks don't band on large print, and darkened edges so the eye is pulled to the performer. The look must be applied IDENTICALLY to all three so the set is consistent. The job, per photo: (1) straighten the tilted frame; (2) auto-tone the flat near-dark exposure and push contrast hard so the two-tone mapping has clean separation between the lit performer and the dark room; (3) desaturate to a neutral grayscale base to kill the mixed warm/cool house-light cast; (4) flatten the grayscale to a single-ink monochromatic base tint in the deep ink-blue shadow ink across the whole frame; (5) lay the warm amber highlight ink over the whole ink-blue base as a whole-image color overlay in a lighten/screen blend so only the brighter (lit-performer) pixels shift toward amber, producing the two-ink noir duotone; (6) apply a subtle whole-image halftone dot texture for the screened-poster feel; (7) add fine film grain; (8) darken the frame edges toward the ink shadow with a whole-image color overlay in a multiply/darken blend so the composition vignettes down and the performer reads as hero; (9) crop to the venue's A2 poster ratio; and separately (10) crop the same treated frame to a 1:1 Instagram tile. Deliver, for each of the three photos, a print-ready high-resolution poster-ratio image (~300 DPI equivalent) and a 1:1 social crop, all carrying the identical treatment so the three read as one matched concert-series set. No text, logo, or layout is required in these deliverables — this is the photographic treatment only; the venue will drop the finished duotone images into their own poster template afterward.

inputs.name: midnight_bass_raw.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: Photoreal phone snapshot of a stand-up double-bass player performing under a single warm stage spotlight in a dim basement jazz club, near-dark room, mixed warm tungsten house light with a cool green-blue spill from an EXIT sign on the left, slightly tilted horizon (about 3 degrees), flat dull underexposed tone, a cluttered background of cabling, an amp, a monitor wedge and a few blurred audience heads. Real skin texture, wood grain on the bass, natural highlight on the strings, candid documentary concert feel, motion-blur on the plucking hand, never CGI-glossy.
inputs.realism_notes: Deliberately imperfect (tilt, flat underexposed tone, mixed warm/cool cast, cluttered dark background) so the straighten + auto-tone + high-contrast prep has real work to do before the two-tone mapping; gpt-image-2 for photoreal people/instrument in low light. Framed loosely with room around the player so it can be cropped to both A2 poster and 1:1 tile.

inputs.name: midnight_vocalist_raw.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: Photoreal phone snapshot of a jazz vocalist leaning into a vintage ribbon microphone on a small stage, eyes closed mid-phrase, dim club lighting with a single warm key and a cool blue rim from a side lamp, faint horizon tilt, flat muddy underexposed tone, distracting background of a drum kit, cymbals catching stray light and a cluttered merch shelf. Real skin pores, flyaway hair, sweat sheen, natural catchlight in the eye, candid documentary feel, no studio gloss, no CGI.
inputs.realism_notes: Second photo of the set; same low-light imperfections and same mixed cast so all three can be driven to a consistent noir duotone; ribbon-mic and closed-eyes phrasing give a strong lit-face-against-dark subject for the two-tone separation to bite into.

inputs.name: midnight_trio_wide_raw.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: Photoreal wide phone snapshot of a three-piece jazz trio mid-set on a low basement stage (double bass, piano, drums), dim warm house wash with a cold blue spill from a stage-left exit, tilted a few degrees, flat dark underexposed tone, busy background of exposed brick, cabling taped to the floor, a PA speaker and the backs of a few audience heads in the foreground. Real instrument texture, natural stage-light falloff, candid concert-documentary realism, slight grain from high ISO, never CGI.
inputs.realism_notes: Third, wider photo; same underexposure and mixed cast plus a busier frame so the identical prep -> desaturate -> duotone -> halftone -> grain -> edge-darken spine applies consistently and the three read as one series.

inputs.name: umbra_brand_inks.txt
inputs.gen_prompt: A short brand note for the venue 'Umbra Loft' listing the two poster inks for the noir duotone as hex/RGB values plus mapping instructions: shadow ink deep ink-blue #10131F (RGB 16,19,31), highlight ink warm amber #E9A23B (RGB 233,162,59). One line: 'The performer should read as amber highlights emerging from deep ink-blue shadows; keep it dark and moody, edges falling to near-black.' Include a note that all three photos must be treated identically for a matched series.
inputs.realism_notes: Supplies the exact two brand colours the duotone steps target (monochromatic-tint hue from the ink-blue shadow, color-overlay RGB from the amber highlight, and the edge-darken shadow RGB), so the two-tone mapping and vignette are specified by the client rather than invented by the tool; also fixes the 'treat all three identically' consistency requirement.

outputs.name: umbra_posters_print_x3
outputs.spec: Three print-ready high-resolution poster-ratio images (A2 portrait, ~300 DPI equivalent), one per performance photo, all carrying the identical noir two-tone treatment: deep ink-blue #10131F shadows lifting to warm amber #E9A23B highlights, subtle halftone dot texture, fine film grain, and edge-darkened vignette; cropped to the A2 poster ratio.

outputs.name: umbra_social_tiles_x3
outputs.spec: Three 1:1 (1080x1080) Instagram tiles of the same three treated frames, matched to the poster treatment, cropped square with the performer centered — must still read as the same noir series at thumbnail size.

connector_workflow.note: Preview the supplied bass photo to confirm the tilt, dull underexposed tone and cluttered dark background before prepping the series.
Level the ~3-degree tilted horizon on the bass photo (first link of the shared treatment spine).
Auto-tone the flat near-dark exposure so the lit performer separates from the black room before the two-tone mapping.
Push contrast hard so the duotone mapping has clean shadow/highlight separation (deep room shadows, bright spotlit performer).
Pull saturation fully down to a neutral grayscale base, killing the mixed warm-house / cool-EXIT color cast before the two-ink map (whole-image, no mask).

Flatten the whole grayscale frame to a single-ink monochromatic base tint in the deep ink-blue shadow ink (hue from #10131F in the brand note) — the standard duotone base ('make it duotone' is this tool's own use case); whole image, no mask.

Lay the warm amber highlight ink (RGB 233,162,59 from #E9A23B) over the WHOLE ink-blue base in a lighten blend so only the brighter (lit-performer) pixels shift toward amber — produces the two-ink noir duotone with no selection/mask (matches the tool's whole-image lighten examples).
Apply a subtle whole-image print-screen halftone dot texture (small radius) so the duotone reads as a screened gig poster.
Add fine film grain so the flat two-tone inks don't band on large-format A2 print.

Darken the frame toward the ink shadow with a whole-image color overlay of the deep ink-blue (RGB 16,19,31) in a multiply/darken blend so the composition falls to near-black at the edges and the spotlit performer reads as hero (whole-image vignette effect, no mask).
Crop/resize the treated bass frame to the A2 portrait poster ratio at ~300 DPI print size (same target applied to all three for a consistent set).
Crop the same treated bass frame to a 1:1 1080x1080 Instagram tile with the player centered.
Start the vocalist photo through the identical treatment spine: level its faint tilt.
Auto-tone the muddy underexposed vocalist frame to match the bass photo's separation.
Push the same hard contrast so the two-tone mapping matches the set.
Desaturate the vocalist frame to the neutral grayscale base to kill its mixed warm-key / cool-rim cast.
Apply the identical deep ink-blue single-ink base tint so the vocalist matches the bass photo's duotone base.
Lay the same warm amber highlight ink over the whole base in the same lighten blend for the matched two-ink noir look.
Apply the same subtle halftone dot texture at the same radius for set consistency.
Add the matching fine film grain.
Apply the same ink-blue multiply/darken edge vignette so the vocalist frame falls to near-black at the edges like the bass frame.
Crop the treated vocalist frame to the matching A2 portrait poster ratio.
Crop the same treated vocalist frame to a 1:1 1080x1080 social tile.
Start the wide trio photo through the identical spine: level its tilt.
Auto-tone the dark wide-frame exposure to match the set's separation.
Push the same hard contrast on the busier wide frame.
Desaturate the trio frame to the neutral grayscale base to kill its mixed warm/cool cast.
Apply the identical deep ink-blue single-ink base tint to the trio frame.
Lay the same amber highlight ink over the whole base in the same lighten blend for the matched two-ink noir look.
Apply the same subtle halftone dot texture at the same radius.
Add the matching fine film grain.
Apply the same ink-blue multiply/darken edge vignette so the trio frame matches the others' near-black falloff.
Crop the treated trio frame to the matching A2 portrait poster ratio so all three posters match.
Crop the same treated trio frame to a 1:1 1080x1080 social tile so all three tiles match.

chaining_note: Strict output->input chaining throughout. Three parallel photo sub-chains each seed from a raw archival photo and run an IDENTICAL 9-step noir-duotone treatment spine, each step consuming only the prior step's output. Bass photo: preview(1, inspection) and straighten(2) -> auto_tone(3) -> brightness_and_contrast(4) -> desaturate(5) -> monochromatic_tint(6, whole-image ink-blue base) -> color_overlay(7, whole-image amber in a lighten blend so only brighter pixels shift) -> halftone(8) -> add_grain(9) -> color_overlay(10, whole-image ink-blue multiply/darken edge vignette) -> then the single treated frame is cropped two ways: A2 poster(11) and 1:1 tile(12). Vocalist mirrors it (13 -> 14 -> 15 -> 16 -> 17 -> 18 -> 19 -> 20 -> 21 -> crop 22/23) and trio mirrors it (24 -> 25 -> 26 -> 27 -> 28 -> 29 -> 30 -> 31 -> 32 -> crop 33/34), each step pulling only its immediate predecessor. The two-ink noir look is built entirely whole-image with NO selection/mask — the duotone emerges purely from monochromatic_tint (ink 1) + a lighten-blend color_overlay (ink 2), and the vignette from a second darken-blend color_overlay, matching those tools' own documented whole-image use cases. All six crop outputs (bass/vocalist/trio poster + tile) are the final deliverables. Nothing after the three raw loads originates outside the chain; every treated deliverable emerges only from the assembled spine.
difficulty_rationale: Long-horizon flagship: 34 connector calls across two Adobe groups (image_* raster look-dev, asset_* preview) and 10 distinct Adobe tools — above the >=10 calls / >=6 distinct-tool floor. Difficulty comes from running three separate low-light concert photos through an IDENTICAL nine-step noir-duotone treatment spine (straighten -> auto-tone -> contrast -> desaturate -> ink-blue tint -> amber overlay -> halftone -> grain -> edge-darken) so they read as one matched concert-series set, then forking each treated frame into an A2 poster crop and a 1:1 social crop. Constructing a genuine two-tone (duotone) hero without any banned capability is the core challenge: the two-ink look is built by real whole-image steps (grayscale base -> monochromatic base tint in ink 1 -> whole-image color-overlay of ink 2 in a lighten/screen blend so only brighter pixels shift -> halftone -> grain -> a second darken-blend overlay for the vignette), not by a text-to-image or generative-fill shortcut and not by any tonal-range selection the tools do not support. It respects every hard limit: no generative fill / generative-expand / reframe-crop / object-removal-with-reconstruction / text-to-image / from-scratch art or logo / upscaling; image_fill_area, image_remove_background and image_select_by_prompt are all unused; no InDesign/Illustrator template is bound and no layout is composed by the connector (this is a pure image-edit treatment, no document_* step); the three photos are treated identically for a consistent set as the brief requires.

reverify.banned_capability_check: None of the banned capabilities are used. No image_crop_and_resize / reframe-crop / reframe-crop; no generative fill of new content; no text-to-image or from-scratch art/logo (there is no logo/text/layout in the deliverables at all — the venue drops the treated images into its own template afterward); no upscaling / super-resolution; no object-removal-with-reconstruction / inpainting (the cluttered dark backgrounds are NOT removed or reconstructed — they are left in and simply rendered dark by the duotone + edge-vignette so the performer reads as hero); no bespoke font/letterform design (font_recommend is not used); no unreachable external accounts. image_remove_background, image_fill_area and image_select_by_prompt do not appear anywhere in the chain.
reverify.notes: Verified against the slot (R08: operation_family 'Photo & Image', output_modality image, workflow_nature edit, vertical arts_culture_music, keywords duotone/two tone/poster/monochrome/treatment) and the AO-14 reference for spec-parity. SLOT-MATCH: category=duotone_poster_fx, workflow_nature=edit, output_modality=image, vertical=arts_culture_music all match; grounded in the real 'MidSight Noir' Freelancer.com candidate (chosen over the logo/e-commerce candidates because it is the only one whose intent is a dark-aesthetic poster + cinematic color-graded photo EDIT with no from-scratch logo/layout, keeping it fully in-palette); fictional IP-clean brand 'Umbra Loft'. COUNTS: connector_workflow has 34 numbered steps -> tool_call_count=34; distinct adobe-server tools = 10 (asset_preview_file, image_auto_straighten, image_apply_auto_tone, image_adjust_brightness_and_contrast, image_adjust_vibrance_and_saturation, image_apply_monochromatic_tint, image_apply_color_overlay, image_apply_halftone, image_add_grain, image_crop_and_resize) -> distinct_adobe_tools=10; tools_used lists exactly these 10 tools. Each potentially-risky step stays in-bounds: (1) The noir duotone is built from real palette primitives, whole-image, with NO selection/mask: image_adjust_vibrance_and_saturation pulls a neutral grayscale base, image_apply_monochromatic_tint flattens to one ink (deep ink-blue #10131F, whose documented use case includes 'make it duotone'), then image_apply_color_overlay lays the second ink (amber RGB 233,162,59) in a lighten blend so only brighter lit-performer pixels shift toward amber — both operate whole-image matching their own documented examples, so no unsupported tonal-range selection is claimed. (2) The edge vignette is a second whole-image image_apply_color_overlay of the ink-blue in a multiply/darken blend; it darkens the whole frame toward the shadow ink and is described as a vignette EFFECT, not a masked region. (3) image_apply_halftone, image_add_grain, image_adjust_brightness_and_contrast and image_apply_auto_tone are palette raster adjusters used whole-image. (4) image_auto_straighten is a geometry fix; image_crop_and_resize is a straight crop to a ratio with no generative expand. (5) asset_preview_file is a read-only inspection. No Express-widget tools (search_design/fill_text/animate_design/change_background_color) and no Canva tools are present; all Adobe steps server=='adobe'. workflow_nature=edit is satisfied: the three SUPPLIED photos are TRANSFORMED into treated image deliverables via non-generative whole-image edits.
